title: Shinji Hamlet
wikitext:
[]: Shinji = Hamlet?             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Mon Jun 15, 2009 9:14 pm Anyone see the similarities? Maybe it's just because I've been obsessed with that play lately, but I see a lot of parallels between them; true, their situations are completely different, but the existential meditations on life and death, the ambivalence, the complexity, nuance, and ambiguity behind their characters and motivations, the oft-paradoxical nature of their psyches and intent, the differing interpretations of them by viewers/readers, and they both end up coming to a kind of apathetic "Let be" conclusion. I've even considered writing an essay on it entitled "To Be or Let Komm Susser Tod", but what does everyone else think?

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Mon Jun 15, 2009 9:17 pm I like it. On a related note, I've been thinking about Shinji's similarities to Josef K. "Nerv wants nothing from you. It receives you when you come and dismisses you when you go." ;)

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Mon Jun 15, 2009 9:29 pm I've been having similar thoughts since we read that play for school in December. I just don't voice these things. Hell, the rest of the characters fit roughly into the cast, especially Asuka as Ophelia. It's enough to make me wonder...

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Jun 16, 2009 12:36 pm Gendo isn't dead and asking Shinji for revenge from beyond the grave.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Tue Jun 16, 2009 12:40 pm But Yui is dead and appears to Shinji in apparitions. It's a gender flip. :P

[]:             Quote Post by Axell » Tue Jun 16, 2009 12:46 pm off the top of my head the only thing i can think of is that everyone dies at the end....sort of

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Tue Jun 16, 2009 2:17 pm I think you're grasping at straws...but that's our Jimbo.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 16, 2009 2:57 pm Toji and Kensuke would make a very good Rosencratz and Gildenstern, I'll give you that much.

[]:             Quote Post by Legendary » Tue Jun 16, 2009 3:20 pm Toji also makes a good Laertes.

[]:             Quote Post by thewayneiac » Tue Jun 16, 2009 4:18 pm I don't really see it. Could you provide some more details? The whole point behind Hamlet is that Hamlet has the "fatal flaw" of indecision, and thus doesn't try to do what he knows must be done, kill his uncle, until it is too late to avert disaster. The key scene is the "chapel" scene, where Hamlet has a clear shot at Claudius, who has demonstrated his guilt by his reaction to the play. At this point the audience realizes that Hamlet is just making excuses when he puts it off, reasoning that if Claudius dies while praying he might not go to hell where he belongs. His failure to act here is what triggers the tragedy. It's really hard to put your finger on any point in Eva where if Shinji had acted decisively the outcome would have been different. In EOE we are already past the point where he could act at all. So, if Shinji were Hamlet, just where would that point be? If only he had kissed Asuka? If only he had found a way to rescue Toji? Would those have somehow averted Third Impact?

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Tue Jun 16, 2009 4:26 pm I'd wager Rosencrantz and Guildernstern Are Dead has more to do with Eva than Hamlet. A bit off topic but, Jimbo, if you're into existential works that exhibit similarities to Evangelion you need to start reading Sartre right the fuck now. Go out and buy or download No Exit (sometimes called Huis Clos ) to start with.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 16, 2009 4:55 pm ZapX wrote: I'd wager Rosencrantz and Guildernstern Are Dead has more to do with Eva than Hamlet.

[]:             Quote Post by ZapX » Tue Jun 16, 2009 5:47 pm NemZ wrote: That's what I was alluding to.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Tue Jun 16, 2009 7:48 pm ZapX wrote: That's what I was responding to.

[]:             Quote Post by Lucretius » Tue Jun 16, 2009 8:20 pm I still think The Trial could be compared with Eva. <_<

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Jun 16, 2009 9:13 pm @ZapX: I've read some of Sartre and more about him and his thought, but never any of his complete works. Like so many others, he's on my "to read" list. thewayneiac wrote: It's really hard to put your finger on any point in Eva where if Shinji had acted decisively the outcome would have been different. In EOE we are already past the point where he could act at all. So, if Shinji were Hamlet, just where would that point be?

[]:             Quote Post by chee » Tue Jun 16, 2009 10:46 pm I second Zap's No Exit recommendation. Gave me NGE flashbacks almost as soon as 10 pages in.

[]:             Quote Post by MugwumpHasNoLiver » Thu Jun 18, 2009 6:08 am Lucretius wrote: I like it. On a related note, I've been thinking about Shinji's similarities to Josef K. "Nerv wants nothing from you. It receives you when you come and dismisses you when you go." ;)

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Sat Jun 27, 2009 11:31 am You might be able to draw comparisons between Evangelion and Hamlet, but you could also draw comparisons between Evangelion and Catcher in the Rye , or even Evangelion and certain aspects of On the Road , so I don't think that comparison was really anything intended on part of the Evangelion crew. While there are some similarities (pseudo-intentional oedipal complexes on the part of an extremely depressed and psychotic protagonist, for instance), the differences I see are too many for me to be convinced it's anything other than a subjective interpretation. For one thing, the very premises of the works at hand are radically different--Revenge, while a theme that does pop up a few times in Evangelion, isn't Eva's whole purpose. Hamlet , on the other hand, is entirely structured around Hamlet's personal vendetta against his treasonous uncle who murdered his dad and usurped his throne. While the play is concerned with Hamlet's ability to accept/cope with reality (seeing his dead ghost dad, doubting that he saw his dead ghost dead, seeing his dead ghost dad again, descending into madness that may-or-may-not have been a mere charade, etc.), Evangelion takes that idea and runs so far with it as to be almost unrecognizable. Eva Yojimbo wrote: Hamlet is well known for its innovations in using the soliloquy to express the inner thoughts of its main character, while in NGE Anno takes to similar plot devices to explore the inner thoughts of his characters [...]

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Jun 27, 2009 6:20 pm Merridian wrote: You might be able to draw comparisons between Evangelion and Hamlet, but you could also draw comparisons between Evangelion and Catcher in the Rye , or even Evangelion and certain aspects of On the Road , so I don't think that comparison was really anything intended on part of the Evangelion crew.

Article: Shinji Hamlet
 Merridian » Sat Jun 27, 2009 11:31 am You might be able to draw comparisons between Evangelion and Hamlet, but you could also draw comparisons between Evangelion and Catcher in the Rye , or even Evangelion and certain aspects of On the Road , so I don't think that comparison was really anything intended on part of the Evangelion crew. While there are some similarities (pseudo-intentional oedipal complexes on the part of an extremely depressed and psychotic protagonist, for instance), the differences I see are too many for me to be convinced it's anything other than a subjective interpretation. For one thing, the very premises of the works at hand are radically different--Revenge, while a theme that does pop up a few times in Evangelion, isn't Eva's whole purpose. Hamlet , on the other hand, is entirely structured around Hamlet's personal vendetta against his treasonous uncle who murdered his dad and usurped his throne. While the play is concerned with Hamlet's ability to accept/cope with reality (seeing his dead ghost dad, doubting that he saw his dead ghost dead, seeing his dead ghost dad again, descending into madness that may-or-may-not have been a mere charade, etc.), Evangelion takes that idea and runs so far with it as to be almost unrecognizable. Eva Yojimbo wrote: Hamlet is well known for its innovations in using the soliloquy to express the inner thoughts of its main character, while in NGE Anno takes to similar plot devices to explore the inner thoughts of his characters [...]
